1:
On Luke 14:1 — “as he went into the house of one of the chief Pharisees to eat bread on the sabbath day… they watched him” — the witnesses mark the hostile vigilance that frames the scene.

The Eastern and Latin fathers note the Lord’s gracious boldness. Cyril: “although our Lord knew the malice of the Pharisees, yet He became their guest, that He might benefit… those who were present.” Bede marks that “the Lord knew the thoughts of men,” and Gregory finds it fitting that “the dropsical man is healed in the Pharisees’ presence, for by the bodily infirmity of the one is expressed the mental disease of the other.” The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the watching: Robertson reads the periphrastic imperfect and the middle voice — “they were themselves watching on the sly… insidiously, with evil intent”; Ellicott identifies the host as “one of the rulers of the Pharisees,” likely a member of the Sanhedrin.

The Reformed Calvin states the design of the miracle to come: not “to abolish the Sabbath, but… to point out, that neither the works of God, nor the duties of charity, violate the holy rest.” The Puritan Henry observes that “the Son of man came eating and drinking,” not declining even the table of enemies, “that… he might do good.”

So the witnesses set the Sabbath-day feast in its tense light. The Lord accepts the invitation of a hostile Pharisee, knowing he is being watched for a slip; and into that watchful malice he brings, deliberately, both a healing and a teaching. The dropsical man set before him — whether a planted trap or a sufferer come for help — becomes the occasion to expose the Pharisees’ perverse reading of the Sabbath. The witnesses thus open a chapter of table-talk in which Christ, surrounded by spies, turns a meal into a school: healing on the Sabbath, teaching humility, rebuking ostentation, and pressing the cost of discipleship — doing good even to those who watched only to accuse him.

So the witnesses frame the chapter as a school kept amid spies. Cyril marks that, knowing the Pharisees’ malice, the Lord yet became their guest “that He might benefit… those who were present”; Gregory, that the dropsical body imaged the diseased mind of the watchers. Into that hostile vigilance Christ brings both a healing and a teaching, turning a Sabbath meal into a lesson on mercy, humility, and the cost of following him — doing good, as Henry says, even to enemies who watched only to accuse.
2:
On Luke 14:2 — “And, behold, there was a certain man before him which had the dropsy” — the witnesses note the sufferer and the physician-evangelist’s precise word.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark Luke’s exactness. Robertson notes “dropsy” (hydropikos) is a “late and medical word… only example of the disease healed by Jesus and recorded”; Ellicott calls it “strictly technical,” another illustration of Luke’s “professional character,” who had “been led to specific inquiries as to the nature of the diseases.” Clarke derives the name from the watery, bloated countenance of the patient. On how the man came there, the witnesses divide gently: Barnes and Clarke suspect “the insidious Pharisee had brought this dropsical man” as a trap, since “they watched him”; Wesley thinks “it does not appear that he was come thither with any insidious design… probably he came, hoping for a cure.”

The verse sets the test in the room. Whether placed there by design or come of his own hope, the dropsical man stands before Jesus on the Sabbath, in the house of his enemies; and his presence forces the question they were waiting to spring — will the Lord heal, and so (as they reckoned) break the Sabbath?

So the witnesses gather the scene with care for its medical truth and its dramatic tension. Luke, the beloved physician, names the disease with technical precision, marking the reality of the man’s misery — the swollen, water-laden body that no human art could quickly cure. And the witnesses note the calculated cruelty, if a trap it was: to use a sick man as bait, caring nothing for his cure but only to catch the Healer. Into that callous watching the Lord brings a genuine compassion; the disease that was meant to ensnare him becomes the occasion of mercy, and the sufferer, real whatever the Pharisees’ design, is about to be made whole.

So the witnesses gather the scene with care for its medical truth and its tension. Luke the physician names the disease with technical precision, marking the reality of the man’s misery; and the witnesses note the calculated cruelty, if a trap it was, of using a sick man as bait. Into that callous watching the Lord brings genuine compassion: the disease meant to ensnare him becomes the occasion of mercy, and the sufferer, real whatever the design, is about to be made whole.
3:
On Luke 14:3 — “And Jesus answering spake unto the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath day?” — the witnesses mark the Lord taking the initiative.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the form and the strategy. Barnes: to “answer” in Scripture often means simply to take up a subject; knowing they watched him, Jesus “proposed the question to them” first. Ellicott marks that here, unlike the earlier Sabbath disputes (Luke 6:6; Matthew 12:9), “our Lord takes the initiative in the controversy,” the scribes and Pharisees treated as one class. Robertson: the dilemma “forestalled any question by them.” Lightfoot details the rabbinic rules that allowed life-saving on the Sabbath.

The Reformed Calvin states the point of the question: “ought the healing of a man to be reckoned among the works which violate the Sabbath?” — and Christ asks it “for the purpose of guarding” himself against their slander. Wesley: he was “answering the thoughts which he saw rising in their hearts.”

So the witnesses admire the Lord’s tactic. He does not wait to be accused; he reads their unspoken design and brings it into the open with a question they cannot safely answer. To say healing is unlawful would condemn their own admitted practice of rescuing a beast; to say it is lawful would justify the very deed they meant to charge against him. The witnesses thus see the Lord turn the trap upon the trappers: by putting the question first, he forces the watchers to declare themselves, and exposes the malice that would forbid a work of mercy on the day God made for blessing.

So the witnesses admire the Lord’s tactic. He does not wait to be accused but reads their design and brings it into the open with a question they cannot safely answer: to call healing unlawful would condemn their own rescue of a beast; to call it lawful would justify the deed they meant to charge. By putting the question first, Ellicott notes, he “takes the initiative,” forcing the watchers to declare themselves and exposing the malice that would forbid a work of mercy on God’s day of blessing.
4:
On Luke 14:4 — “And they held their peace. And he took him, and healed him, and let him go” — the witnesses mark the silence of the critics and the deed of mercy.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the silence as defeat. Barnes: “they could not say it was not lawful, for the law did not forbid it… Here was the time… to make objections… and as they made none then, they could not… afterward.” Clarke: “they were silent” because any answer “would condemn themselves.” Robertson notes the verb “took hold of him” (epilabomenos) — the only instance of its use in a healing — and that “let him go” means he “dismissed” the man from the company, “to get him away from these critics.” Ellicott: the cure was “effected by actual contact.”

The verse joins the rulers’ helpless silence to the Lord’s swift compassion. Having put the question and received no answer, Christ acts: he lays hold of the man, heals him, and sends him away — out of reach of the watching enemies. The deed is done before they can object, and their own silence has forfeited the right to object after.

So the witnesses see the controversy settled by act as well as word. The Pharisees’ silence was an admission they would not make aloud; the healing was a mercy they could not gainsay. The Lord’s tender dismissal of the man — getting him safely away from those who had used his sickness as a snare — the witnesses read as a touch of pastoral care: the sufferer is not only cured but shielded. And the whole scene becomes a living argument against the Pharisees’ Sabbath, proving by deed what the question had proved by logic — that to heal on the Sabbath is not to break it but to honour the God of mercy whose day it is.

So the witnesses see the controversy settled by act as well as word. The Pharisees’ silence was an admission they would not make aloud; the healing a mercy they could not gainsay. The Lord’s tender dismissal of the man — getting him safely away from those who had used his sickness as a snare — the witnesses read as pastoral care: the sufferer is not only cured but shielded. The deed proves by act what the question proved by logic, that to heal on the Sabbath is to honour, not break, the God of mercy whose day it is.
5:
On Luke 14:5 — “Which of you shall have an ass or an ox fallen into a pit, and will not straightway pull him out on the sabbath day?” — the witnesses mark the unanswerable argument from their own practice.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the homely force. Barnes and Ellicott set it beside Luke 13:15 and Matthew 12:11: if it was lawful to save an ox on the Sabbath, much more to save a man. Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic rulings that allowed dispensing with the Sabbath “for the preservation of a beast.” Robertson notes the textual variant — some manuscripts read “a son or an ox” (huios), others “an ass or an ox” (onos), the two looking alike in the uncials.

The Reformed Calvin marks the aim: Christ shows “he did nothing inconsistent with the observance of the Sabbath,” not so much to instruct men who were “too much blinded by virulent hatred to yield,” as “to protect himself against their slanders.”

So the witnesses see the Lord convict the Pharisees out of their own custom. They would, without scruple, haul a fallen beast from a pit on the Sabbath, counting it mercy and necessity; how then could they fault the rescue of a suffering man? The argument runs from the less to the greater — from the ox to the image of God — and it left them, as the next verse says, without reply. The witnesses note the exposure of their inconsistency: they cared, in their hearts, more for a beast’s safety than a man’s, and so condemned in Christ what they daily allowed themselves. The Sabbath, rightly kept, is the day of mercy; and the very rule they pressed against him recoiled upon their own practice.

So the witnesses see the Lord convict the Pharisees out of their own custom. They would, without scruple, haul a fallen beast from a pit on the Sabbath; how then fault the rescue of a suffering man? The argument runs from the ox to the image of God, and left them speechless. The witnesses mark the exposure of their inconsistency — they cared more, in their hearts, for a beast’s safety than a man’s — and Calvin’s point, that Christ spoke chiefly to guard himself against their slander, knowing the blinded would not yield to argument.
6:
On Luke 14:6 — “And they could not answer him again to these things” — the witnesses mark the silencing of the critics.

The voices here are brief. The Reformed Gill: the “justice, equity, mercy, and humanity” of the Lord’s reasoning, being agreeable to their own practice, left their “mouths… shut up.” The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the Greek as “a little more emphatic — ‘they had no power, they were powerless to answer him’”; Robertson: “they could not take up the argument and were helpless. They hated to admit that they cared more for an ox or ass or even a son than for this poor dropsical man.”

The verse seals the Sabbath controversy. The Pharisees, who had watched to accuse, are left without a word; their own custom has condemned them, and they cannot answer. The argument from the fallen beast, joined to the healing already done, has stripped them of every objection.

So the witnesses close the scene on the Pharisees’ helpless silence — a silence more eloquent than speech. They could not deny the mercy, nor justify their malice, nor own the inconsistency the Lord had exposed. The witnesses read in it the perpetual defeat of a religion of mere rule when confronted by the law of love: the men who would forbid a healing on the Sabbath are reduced to speechlessness by the reminder that they would themselves rescue a brute. And the silence is not repentance but defeat — for, as the verses following show, they go on to seek the chief seats and to grudge the gospel feast, their pride untouched by the argument that had stopped their mouths.

So the witnesses close the Sabbath controversy on the Pharisees’ helpless silence — more eloquent than speech. They could not deny the mercy, justify the malice, nor own the inconsistency exposed. Robertson marks that they “hated to admit” they cared more for an ox than for the dropsical man. The witnesses read in it the perpetual defeat of a religion of mere rule before the law of love; yet the silence is not repentance but defeat, for they go on, in the verses following, to grasp the chief seats and grudge the gospel feast, their pride untouched.
7:
On Luke 14:7 — “he put forth a parable to those which were bidden, when he marked how they chose out the chief rooms” — the witnesses mark the turn from Sabbath to humility.

The Eastern and Latin fathers draw out the lesson against vainglory. Ambrose: after the dropsical man, “humility is taught, when at the nuptial feast the desire of the highest place is forbidden.” Cyril: to seek honours unfitting for us “indicates rashness,” while the lowly are “crowned with honours.” Theophylact warns that none should deem the precept trifling, for vainglory is no slight disease — a physician who healed the gout but scorned a toothache would be no true physician. Bede reads the “parable” mystically of the Church, where one must “not exalt himself… by boasting of his merits.”

The grammatical-historical commentators note that “parable” here means a precept or rule (Barnes), and that Robertson reads the imperfect “they were picking out” the chief couches. The Reformed Calvin marks the ambition rife among the Pharisees, who, “desiring to exercise a haughty dominion,” cherished envy over the highest seats.

So the witnesses see the Lord turn from the healing to the manners of the feast, reading a deeper sickness in the guests than in the patient. Watching them scramble for the best places, he frames a precept on humility — not mere table-etiquette, the fathers insist, but a cure for the soul’s vainglory. The very men who had grudged a Sabbath mercy now jostle for honour; and the Lord, who reads hearts, makes their petty ambition the text of a lesson that reaches to the kingdom of God. The witnesses thus open the teaching on self-abasement and exaltation that the chapter will press — that the way up, in God’s house, is down.

So the witnesses see the Lord turn from the healing to the manners of the feast, reading a deeper sickness in the guests than in the patient. Watching them scramble for the best places, he frames a precept on humility — not mere etiquette, the fathers insist, but a cure for the soul’s vainglory, which Theophylact will not call a slight disease. The men who grudged a Sabbath mercy now jostle for honour; and the Lord makes their petty ambition the text of a lesson that reaches to the kingdom of God, where the way up is down.
8:
On Luke 14:8 — “When thou art bidden of any man to a wedding, sit not down in the highest room; lest a more honourable man than thou be bidden of him” — the witnesses mark the prudence and the deeper humility taught.

The grammatical-historical commentators read both the social and the spiritual. Barnes: “our Saviour did not consider the courtesies of life to be beneath his notice,” yet “his chief design… was… to reprove” pride. Ellicott notes “recline not” (the Eastern posture), and that “the common practice was for the guests to seat themselves.” Robertson parses the ingressive aorist “sit not down.” Lightfoot supplies rabbinic anecdotes of the wise man seated next the prince in honour of his wisdom.

The verse begins the precept with a homely caution. To grasp at the highest seat is to risk the shame of being displaced when a worthier guest arrives; the prudent course is not to presume. Yet, as the fathers saw, the rule of the table is a parable of the soul: the self-exalting will be put down.

So the witnesses find in the wedding-feast a school of humility. The Lord clothes a deep truth in a common scene: the man who claims the chief place before his host has assigned it courts public disgrace. Beneath the social wisdom lies the spiritual law — that to exalt oneself is to invite abasement, in the kingdom no less than at the banquet. The witnesses note how the Lord stoops to teach by the very manners of the meal, dignifying ordinary courtesy by making it the vehicle of eternal instruction: he who would be honoured must not seize honour, but leave it to be given by the Master of the feast.

So the witnesses find in the wedding-feast a school of humility. The Lord clothes a deep truth in a common scene: the man who claims the chief place before his host assigns it courts public disgrace. Beneath the social wisdom lies the spiritual law — that to exalt oneself is to invite abasement, in the kingdom no less than at the banquet. The witnesses note how he stoops to teach by the very manners of the meal: he who would be honoured must not seize honour but leave it to be given by the Master of the feast.
9:
On Luke 14:9 — “And he that bade thee and him come and say to thee, Give this man place; and thou begin with shame to take the lowest room” — the witnesses mark the humiliation of the self-exalting.

The voices here are few. The grammatical-historical Robertson reads “shalt begin with shame” (arxe meta aischynes) as “the moment of embarrassment,” forced “to hold down the lowest place, all the intermediate ones being taken.” Ellicott meets the difficulty that the precept seems to commend a “calculating rather than a real humility,” offering the explanation that social precepts, as in the Book of Proverbs, “start naturally” from prudential grounds and rise to higher ones.

The verse paints the sharp reversal. The man who took the high seat is publicly told to yield it, and must make his way, before all the guests, to the only place left — the lowest. The shame is the greater for being public, and the descent the more galling for being from the top to the bottom, past every middle place now filled.

So the witnesses dwell on the disgrace of presumption. The Lord draws the picture vividly: not a quiet correction but a public unseating, the proud guest sent down in the sight of all to the very foot of the table. The lesson, the witnesses note, is not a cynical calculation but a true reading of how exaltation of self ends — in abasement, and that the more bitter for being self-inflicted and openly seen. The man could have had honour by waiting for it; by seizing it he earns shame. The parable thus warns every ambitious heart that the seat grasped is the seat lost, and that pride’s reward is a public descent.

So the witnesses dwell on the disgrace of presumption. The Lord draws it vividly: not a quiet correction but a public unseating, the proud guest sent down before all to the very foot of the table, past every middle place now filled. The lesson is no cynical calculation but a true reading of how self-exaltation ends — in abasement, the more bitter for being self-inflicted and openly seen. The man could have had honour by waiting; by seizing it he earns shame. The seat grasped is the seat lost, and pride’s reward a public descent.
10:
On Luke 14:10 — “But when thou art bidden, go and sit down in the lowest room… then shalt thou have worship in the presence of them that sit at meat with thee” — the witnesses mark the honour that comes by humility.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the reversal. Barnes: to take the lowest seat shows “you are not… greedy of that honour which may properly belong to you,” and “they who are sitting with you shall treat you with respect” — “worship” here meaning honour. Ellicott notes the host’s address — “Friend” — is the word of “confidence and affection” (John 15:14), not the cooler term of Matthew 22:12: “he is welcomed” warmly. Robertson parses the imperatives of reclining.

The verse completes the precept with its happier side. The humble guest, who takes the lowest place, is called up higher by the host before all the company, and so receives, freely given, the honour the proud man grasped and lost. The way to be exalted is to abase oneself and leave the lifting to another.

So the witnesses find in the lowest seat the path to true honour. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem hears beneath it the table God spreads for his own (Psalm 23:5). The lesson is not that humility is a clever stratagem for honour — that would be pride in disguise — but that genuine self-abasement is the very condition on which God, the Master of the feast, delights to exalt. The honour the humble receive is not seized but bestowed, and bestowed publicly, “in the presence of them that sit at meat.” The witnesses thus set the gracious “Friend, go up higher” against the shameful “Give this man place,” and read in the contrast the whole law of the kingdom: the lowly are lifted, and the honour that lasts is the honour God gives.

So the witnesses find in the lowest seat the path to true honour. The lesson is not that humility is a clever stratagem — that would be pride in disguise — but that genuine self-abasement is the very condition on which God, the Master of the feast, delights to exalt. The honour the humble receive is not seized but bestowed, and bestowed publicly. The gracious “Friend, go up higher” (a word, Ellicott notes, of affection) stands against the shameful “Give this man place,” and in the contrast the witnesses read the whole law of the kingdom: the lowly are lifted, and the honour that lasts is the honour God gives.
11:
On Luke 14:11 — “For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted” — the witnesses mark the great law that crowns the parable.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it as a universal divine principle. Barnes: it is “a part of God’s regular plan to abase the proud… and show his favours to those who are poor and needy.” Clarke: “this is the unchangeable conduct of God: he is ever abasing the proud, and giving grace… to the humble.” Robertson notes it is “one of the repeated sayings of Jesus” (Luke 18:14; Matthew 23:12); Ellicott, its echo in 1 Peter 5:5.

The Reformed Calvin makes plain that the subject is not mere table-manners but the soul before God: “he does not state what usually happens in… ordinary life… but declares that God will be their Judge, who… humbleth their haughtiness” (James 4:6).

So the witnesses lift the precept from the feast to the throne of God. What began as counsel about seats ends as a law of the kingdom and a revelation of the divine character: God is the enemy of the proud and the friend of the lowly. The verse, repeated so often on the Lord’s lips, is the hinge of the whole teaching — self-exaltation invites abasement, self-humbling invites exaltation, and the Judge who applies the rule is God himself. The witnesses thus read in the wedding-parable a window into heaven: the same God who calls the humble guest up higher will, at the last, abase every proud and exalt every lowly heart, and the wise will order their lives by this unchangeable law of his.

So the witnesses lift the precept from the feast to the throne of God. What began as counsel about seats ends as a law of the kingdom and a revelation of the divine character: God is the enemy of the proud and the friend of the lowly. The saying, so often on the Lord’s lips ({r("Matthew 23:12","Matthew 23:12")}; {r("1 Peter 5:5","1 Peter 5:5")}), is the hinge of the whole teaching; and Calvin marks that the Judge who applies it is God himself. The same God who calls the humble guest up higher will, at the last, abase every proud and exalt every lowly heart.
12:
On Luke 14:12 — “When thou makest a dinner or a supper, call not thy friends… lest they also bid thee again, and a recompence be made thee” — the witnesses mark the turn from guest to host.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the precept against self-interested hospitality. Theophylact: having taught the invited humility, the Lord now “rewards by His advice the inviter, guarding him against making a feast to gain the favour of men.” Chrysostom distinguishes the natural and moral bonds — kindred, neighbours — that the Lord names. Bede is careful: “brothers… and friends… are not forbidden, as though it were a crime to entertain one another,” but such feasting is shown “to fail in meriting the reward of everlasting life.”

The Reformed and Wesleyan voices guard the sense from excess. Calvin: those who read it as “an absolute condemnation of entertainments given by relatives and friends… take away… civility” — Christ “did not intend to dissuade us from every thing courteous.” Clarke: he “condemns those entertainments… given to the rich, either to flatter them, or to procure a similar return.” Robertson notes the present imperative — prohibiting “the habit of inviting only friends.”

So the witnesses balance the precept carefully. The Lord does not forbid ordinary hospitality among kin and friends, which is a courtesy of human life; he forbids the calculating hospitality that gives only where it expects a return, and so makes a feast a transaction. The fathers and the Reformers agree: the sin is not in entertaining the rich but in entertaining only those who can repay, with no thought of the poor who cannot. The witnesses thus read a warning against a generosity that is really self-interest — a giving that looks for its wage in kind — and a call, in the next verse, to a higher charity that gives where no recompense can follow, looking for its reward to God.

So the witnesses balance the precept carefully. The Lord does not forbid ordinary hospitality among kin and friends, a courtesy of human life; he forbids the calculating hospitality that gives only where it expects a return, making a feast a transaction. Bede is careful that kindred “are not forbidden, as though it were a crime,” and Calvin that Christ would not “take away… civility.” The sin is not in entertaining the rich but in entertaining only those who can repay, with no thought of the poor — a generosity that is really self-interest, looking for its wage in kind.
13:
On Luke 14:13 — “But when thou makest a feast, call the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind” — the witnesses mark the charity that looks for no return.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the four classes who cannot repay. Barnes: “the poor… the maimed… deprived of any member… who have not the use of them.” Robertson observes the absence of the article in the Greek — “poor people, maimed folks, lame people, blind people” — the whole company of the helpless. Ellicott marks that the precise “form of obedience… must vary with the… phases of social life”: relief “given privately, thoughtfully, discriminatively” may best fulfil the spirit.

The verse sets the higher charity against the calculating hospitality just forbidden. Where the world feasts those who can feast it back, the disciple is to gather the very people who can give nothing in return — the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind. The choice of guests is itself the test of motive: to invite the helpless is to give for God’s sake and not for repayment.

So the witnesses read in the four named classes the pattern of a love that imitates God’s. The Lord names not the agreeable and the influential but the broken and the burdensome, those whom self-interest would never invite. To feast such is to part with the hope of recompense from men, and so to give purely; and the very act, the witnesses note, mirrors the grace of the gospel, which gathers to its supper the poor and lame who have nothing to bring. The verse thus turns hospitality into charity, and charity into a likeness of God — bidding the disciple open his table, as God opens his, to those who can only receive.

So the witnesses read in the four named classes the pattern of a love that imitates God’s. The Lord names not the agreeable and influential but the broken and burdensome, whom self-interest would never invite. To feast such is to part with the hope of recompense, and so to give purely; and the act mirrors the grace of the gospel, which gathers to its supper the poor and lame who have nothing to bring. The verse turns hospitality into charity, and charity into a likeness of God — bidding the disciple open his table, as God opens his, to those who can only receive.
14:
On Luke 14:14 — “And thou shalt be blessed; for they cannot recompense thee: for thou shalt be recompensed at the resurrection of the just” — the witnesses mark the heavenly reward of unselfish giving.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the “resurrection of the just.” Ellicott marks this as “the first occurrence of the word ‘resurrection’ in our Lord’s teaching,” and that Christ here “accepted the fundamental doctrine” of the Pharisees, furnishing a precedent for Paul (Acts 23:6). Barnes: the just “shall be raised from the dead” and God himself will be the recompenser; Clarke: because the poor “cannot make you a recompense… God will consider himself your debtor.”

The Reformed Calvin draws the principle: he “pronounces those to be” blessed “who exercise liberality without any expectation of earthly reward; for they manifestly look to God” — while those who “keep in view their own advantage… have no right to expect a reward from God.”

So the witnesses find in the verse the motive that crowns the charity just enjoined. The very inability of the poor to repay, which the calculating host would shun, is what makes the gift blessed: it cannot be recompensed by men, and so will be recompensed by God, at the resurrection of the just. The witnesses note the great exchange the Lord proposes — to forgo a small return now for a sure reward then; to lend, as it were, to God by giving to his poor. And they mark, with Ellicott, the solemn weight of the new word: the resurrection, here first named in Christ’s teaching, is set forth as the day of reckoning when unselfish love, unpaid on earth, shall receive its wage from the hand of God.

So the witnesses find in the verse the motive that crowns the charity enjoined. The very inability of the poor to repay, which the calculating host would shun, is what makes the gift blessed: unrecompensed by men, it will be recompensed by God at the resurrection of the just. The witnesses note the great exchange — to forgo a small return now for a sure reward then, lending, as it were, to God by giving to his poor — and, with Ellicott, the weight of the new word: the resurrection, here first named in Christ’s teaching, set forth as the day when unselfish love receives its wage.
15:
On Luke 14:15 — “Blessed is he that shall eat bread in the kingdom of God” — the witnesses weigh the guest’s pious exclamation that occasions the parable of the great supper.

The Eastern and Latin fathers probe the saying. Eusebius and Augustine see a man who, hearing of the “resurrection of the just,” sighs after a bliss he imagines distant — “that Bread of the kingdom… lay before him,” for Christ is “the living bread which came down from heaven” (John 6:51); “open not your mouth, but your heart.” Cyril judges the man “carnal, and a careless hearer,” who “thought the reward of the saints was to be bodily.” The grammatical-historical Robertson calls it a “pious platitude… whether due to ignorance or hypocrisy,” the rabbis’ common figure of a banquet for heaven’s bliss; Barnes and Clarke note the Jewish dream of a “temporal” Messianic feast.

The verse marks the hinge from the table-talk to the parable. A guest, moved by the Lord’s words of the resurrection-reward, voices the conventional Jewish blessing — happy the man who shall feast in the kingdom — assuming complacently that he and his kind are sure of a place.

So the witnesses read the exclamation as the spark of the great parable. Whether the man spoke from honest devotion or smug presumption, his words gave the Lord his text: yes, blessed is he that shall eat in the kingdom — but who will? The fathers fasten on the irony that the Bread of the kingdom sat at the very table, unrecognised; and the parable to follow will expose how many, bidden to the feast, refuse it for the trifles of this world. The witnesses thus see the Lord take up a pious commonplace and turn it into a searching warning: the kingdom’s supper is spread and the invitation gone out, but the complacent who assume their place may find themselves shut out, while the poor and the outcast come in.

So the witnesses read the exclamation as the spark of the great parable. Whether spoken from honest devotion or smug presumption, the words gave the Lord his text: yes, blessed is he that shall eat in the kingdom — but who will? The fathers fasten on the irony that the Bread of the kingdom ({r("John 6:51","John 6:51")}) sat at the very table, unrecognised. The parable to follow will expose how many, bidden to the feast, refuse it for the trifles of this world, and how the complacent who assume their place may find themselves shut out, while the poor and outcast come in.
16:
On Luke 14:16 — “A certain man made a great supper, and bade many” — the witnesses open the parable of the great supper.

The grammatical-historical commentators set out the interpretation. Barnes: “the man who made the supper… is… designed to represent God; the supper, the provisions… for the salvation of men; and the invitation, the offers… made to men.” Ellicott marks this as “the first occurrence of the ‘feast’ imagery” in Christ’s teaching, “suggested by the occasion,” and afterward expanded in Matthew 22:1; Robertson notes the inchoative imperfect “was on the point of making,” and that Christ takes up “the conventional remark of the guest” to show that such complacency “was no guarantee of godliness.” Bede adds that the man “represents God the Father.”

The verse answers the guest’s pious exclamation with a parable. Yes, there is a great supper in the kingdom; and yes, many are bidden. The host is God, the feast his salvation, the invitation the gospel call — freely and widely sent. The stage is set for the surprising sequel: that those first bidden will refuse.

So the witnesses read the opening of the parable as both promise and warning. The promise is in the “great supper” and the “many” bidden: God has prepared a rich provision and issued a generous invitation. The warning lies in what follows. The guest had assumed that to be bidden was to be sure of a seat; the parable will show that a invitation may be despised, and a feast prepared in vain for those who will not come. The witnesses thus see the Lord take the Jewish hope of the kingdom-banquet and turn it into a probing question: the supper is spread and the call gone out — but will the bidden come, or will they, for the things of earth, refuse the bread of heaven?

So the witnesses read the opening as both promise and warning. The promise is in the “great supper” and the “many” bidden: God has prepared a rich provision and issued a generous invitation. The warning lies in what follows. The guest had assumed that to be bidden was to be sure of a seat; the parable will show that an invitation may be despised, and a feast prepared in vain for those who will not come. The Lord takes the Jewish hope of the kingdom-banquet and turns it into a probing question: will the bidden come, or refuse the bread of heaven for the things of earth?
17:
On Luke 14:17 — “And sent his servant at supper time to say to them that were bidden, Come; for all things are now ready” — the witnesses read the second summons and its messenger.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the custom and the meaning. Barnes and Robertson note the Eastern practice of a second summons when the feast was ready, a refusal of which Tristram says “is equivalent… to a declaration of war.” Ellicott reads the servant as “the representative of the whole order of prophets and apostles… sent to invite men to the Kingdom,” the “supper time” being “the time of our Lord’s coming.” The Reformed Gill takes the servant of John the Baptist, or of Christ himself, or of the gospel ministry, proclaiming the kingdom at hand.

The verse marks the urgency and the readiness of the gospel call. The feast is prepared; nothing is wanting; the servant goes out with the final word, “Come; for all things are now ready.” The invitation is not a vague promise but a present summons to a banquet actually spread.

So the witnesses find in the second summons the very heart of the gospel offer. “All things are now ready” — the provision is complete, the welcome open, nothing remains but for the bidden to come. The servant, the witnesses agree, stands for the messengers God has sent, from the prophets to the apostles, with the one urgent word: the kingdom is prepared, enter it now. And the gravity of refusing is underscored by the custom — to spurn the second summons was a deadly affront. The witnesses thus set the freeness and fullness of the gospel against the excuses to follow: a feast wholly furnished, a call wholly gracious, met by hearts that, for the smallest reasons, will not come.

So the witnesses find in the second summons the very heart of the gospel offer. “All things are now ready” — the provision complete, the welcome open, nothing remaining but for the bidden to come. The servant, they agree, stands for the messengers God has sent, from the prophets to the apostles, with the one urgent word: the kingdom is prepared, enter it now. And the gravity of refusing is underscored by the custom — to spurn the second summons was a deadly affront. The witnesses thus set the freeness and fullness of the gospel against the excuses to follow.
18:
On Luke 14:18 — “And they all with one consent began to make excuse. The first said, I have bought a piece of ground, and I must needs go and see it” — the witnesses mark the strange unanimity of refusal.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the suspicious agreement. Robertson: “with one consent” (apo mias) “looked like a conspiracy, for each one in his turn did the same thing”; the verb “to make excuse” (paraiteisthai) means to beg off, decline. Lightfoot marks the absurdity of the first plea — an “unseasonable” time, near nightfall, to inspect new-bought ground. Barnes draws the lesson: “sinners sometimes plead that they are under a necessity to neglect” the gospel, when the necessity is of their own making.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley analyses the three pleas: “one… pleads only his own will, I go; another, a pretended necessity, I must needs go; the third, impossibility, I cannot come… all of them perish by things in themselves lawful.”

So the witnesses read in the excuses the heart of unbelief. The thing that keeps the first guest away is not wickedness but a field — a lawful possession made an unlawful pretext. The unanimity is telling: though their words differ, their hearts agree in one thing, that the supper of the kingdom matters less to them than their own affairs. The witnesses mark, with Wesley, that all three perish “by things in themselves lawful” — land, oxen, marriage — for it is not gross sin but the love of the world that crowds out the call of God. The verse thus exposes the commonest ruin of souls: not open rejection of the gospel, but a preoccupation with earthly good that will not spare the time to come.

So the witnesses read in the excuses the heart of unbelief. The thing that keeps the first guest away is not wickedness but a field — a lawful possession made an unlawful pretext. The unanimity is telling: though their words differ, their hearts agree that the supper of the kingdom matters less than their own affairs. The witnesses mark, with Wesley, that all three perish “by things in themselves lawful”; it is not gross sin but the love of the world that crowds out the call of God — the commonest ruin of souls, a preoccupation with earthly good that will not spare the time to come.
19:
On Luke 14:19 — “And another said, I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I go to prove them: I pray thee have me excused” — the witnesses mark the second excuse.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the triviality. Barnes: “this excuse was very trifling. He could as easily have tried them at any other time… He was selfish; just as all sinners are.” Robertson: “he could have tested them before buying. The oxen would not run away.” Ellicott notes “five yoke” was within reach of “any peasant farmer of moderate competence” (cf. Elisha’s twelve, 1 Kings 19:19).

The verse adds a second self-made necessity. The man has bought his oxen and must, he says, go to prove them — an errand that could have waited, urged at the very hour the feast was ready. Like the first, the excuse dresses up a preference as a duty: he would rather see to his oxen than sit at the supper.

So the witnesses see in the oxen another emblem of the world’s engrossing business. The man is not wicked; he is busy — busy with a lawful trade, made the reason for declining a gracious call. The witnesses note how flimsy the plea is when weighed against the feast refused: the oxen would keep; the proving could wait; only the heart’s preference made the errand seem urgent. The fathers (as the next verse will gather) read the oxen of the bodily senses or earthly cares; the plainer lesson the witnesses press is the same as before — that the cares and possessions of this life, harmless in themselves, become deadly when they are preferred to the kingdom of God, and the soul finds in its daily business an excuse for refusing heaven.

So the witnesses see in the oxen another emblem of the world’s engrossing business. The man is not wicked but busy — busy with a lawful trade made the reason for declining a gracious call. The plea is flimsy when weighed against the feast refused: the oxen would keep, the proving could wait; only the heart’s preference made the errand urgent. The plainer lesson the witnesses press is the same as before — that the cares and possessions of this life, harmless in themselves, become deadly when preferred to the kingdom, and the soul finds in its daily business an excuse for refusing heaven.
20:
On Luke 14:20 — “And another said, I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come” — the witnesses mark the third and bluntest excuse.

The grammatical-historical commentators note its rudeness and its root. Barnes: “this was the most trifling excuse of all… the love of earthly relatives and friends often takes off the affections from God.” Robertson: “I cannot come” is “less polite than the others”; the law excused a newly married man from war (Deuteronomy 24:5), “but not from social courtesy.” Ellicott marks that the Law allowed marriage, a new house, or a vineyard as grounds for exemption from military service (Deuteronomy 20:5–7), “but the sin… was that they treated the invitation to the feast as though it were as burdensome as a military conscription.”

The verse completes the trio of excuses with the curtest of all. The third guest does not even ask to be excused; he simply declares, “I cannot come.” His plea — a new marriage — is a lawful joy turned into a flat refusal, as though a feast were an intolerable burden.

So the witnesses read in the three excuses a rising scale of preoccupation and rudeness: a field, oxen, a wife — property, business, affection — each a lawful thing made the ground of refusing God. The third is the boldest, pleading not even a delay but an impossibility, where in truth the man might well have come, and brought his wife with him. The witnesses note how the dearest earthly good, the love of family, can most strongly draw the heart from heaven; and they mark the affront of treating the King’s gracious supper as a thing one “cannot” attend. The parable thus lays bare the threefold ruin of souls — possessions, labour, and love — all good in their place, all damning when preferred to the call of God.

So the witnesses read in the three excuses a rising scale of preoccupation and rudeness: a field, oxen, a wife — property, business, affection — each a lawful thing made the ground of refusing God. The third is the boldest, pleading not even a delay but an impossibility, where in truth the man might well have come, and brought his wife with him. The witnesses note how the dearest earthly good, the love of family, can most strongly draw the heart from heaven; and they mark the affront of treating the King’s gracious supper as a thing one “cannot” attend.
21:
On Luke 14:21 — “the master of the house being angry said… Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in hither the poor, and the maimed, and the halt, and the blind” — the witnesses mark the anger and the new invitation.

The grammatical-historical commentators read both. Robertson notes the ingressive aorist “being angry” (orgistheis), and “quickly” — “the dinner is ready and no time is to be lost.” Ellicott marks that the righteous indignation is “more strongly emphasised” in Matthew 22:6, and that the “streets and lanes” gather the city’s neglected. The Reformed Gill applies it to the gospel’s turning: the first-bidden Jews having rejected the word, the call goes to the despised — the poor and outcast of Israel.

The verse turns the parable on its hinge. The host’s anger at the contemptuous refusal does not empty the feast but fills it from another quarter: the very people the proud would never invite — the poor, maimed, halt, blind — the same classes the Lord had just named for charity, now made the honoured guests of God.

So the witnesses read in the new invitation the grace and the justice of God together. Justice, in the anger that bars the scornful who would not come; grace, in the haste to fill their places with the wretched of the streets. The witnesses note the fitting irony: the supper rejected by the self-satisfied is given to the helpless, who have nothing and can refuse nothing. The four named classes — the very guests the disciple was bidden to feast — become the picture of those whom the gospel gathers: the poor in spirit, the broken, the despised, who know their need and so receive the call. The feast of the kingdom, spurned by the first, is crowded with the last.

So the witnesses read in the new invitation the grace and justice of God together: justice, in the anger that bars the scornful who would not come; grace, in the haste to fill their places with the wretched of the streets. The fitting irony, they note, is that the supper rejected by the self-satisfied is given to the helpless, who have nothing and can refuse nothing. The four named classes — the very guests the disciple was bidden to feast — become the picture of those whom the gospel gathers: the poor in spirit, the broken, the despised, who know their need and so receive the call.
22:
On Luke 14:22 — “And the servant said, Lord, it is done as thou hast commanded, and yet there is room” — the witnesses mark the abundance of the feast.

The voices here are brief but glad. The grammatical-historical Barnes turns the words into gospel: “What a glorious declaration is this… There yet is room. Millions have been saved, but there yet is room. Millions have been invited… but heaven is not yet full.” Clarke notes the Eastern custom of borrowing a larger hall when the guests are many. Robertson: “the servant knew the Master wished the places to be filled.”

The verse marks the gospel’s spaciousness. The poor and maimed have been gathered, the command obeyed — “and yet there is room.” The feast is not filled by the first refusers nor even by the city’s outcasts; the table of God is larger than all who have come, and the invitation must go wider still.

So the witnesses hear in “yet there is room” one of the most gracious words of the gospel. The supper of the kingdom is no narrow board but a banquet of immeasurable welcome; however many come, there is place for more. The witnesses, with Barnes, read it as a standing answer to every fear of exclusion: heaven is not full, the door is not shut, the call is not spent. The same abundance that gathered the poor of the streets reaches out, in the next verse, to the highways beyond the city — to the Gentiles, the strangers, the lost. The verse thus sets the largeness of God’s grace against the smallness of men’s excuses: those who would not come have lost nothing of the feast, for the King’s house has room for all who will.

So the witnesses hear in “yet there is room” one of the most gracious words of the gospel. The supper of the kingdom is no narrow board but a banquet of immeasurable welcome; however many come, there is place for more. Barnes reads it as a standing answer to every fear of exclusion: heaven is not full, the door not shut, the call not spent. The same abundance that gathered the poor of the streets reaches out, in the next verse, to the highways beyond — the largeness of God’s grace set against the smallness of men’s excuses.
23:
On Luke 14:23 — “Go out into the highways and hedges, and compel them to come in, that my house may be filled” — the witnesses mark the third and widest invitation, and the meaning of “compel.”

The grammatical-historical commentators read the widening scope. Barnes and Ellicott: beyond the city’s streets, the highways and hedges gather “the tramps and the squatters who had no home” — in the application, the Gentiles. Robertson: “the heathen are to be invited this time.” On “compel” (anankason), all are emphatic that no force is meant: Clarke, “prevail on them by the most earnest entreaties”; Robertson, “by persuasion of course. There is no thought of compulsion.” The Reformed Calvin: the master would “make use, as it were, of violence for compelling the attendance of the poor” — that is, an urgency of love.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley states it for the conscience of the Church: “compel them… with all the violence of love, and the force of God’s word. Such compulsion, and such only, in matters of religion, was used by Christ and his apostles.”

So the witnesses read the verse as the gospel reaching to the ends of the earth, and they are at one in fencing it from abuse. The “highways and hedges” are the Gentile world, the homeless and far-off; and they too must be brought in, that God’s house be filled. The “compel,” the witnesses insist with one voice, is the constraint not of force but of love — the earnest, importunate persuasion of the gospel, which presses the unwilling with the urgency of God’s welcome. The word that persecutors have wrested to justify violence the witnesses reclaim for its true sense: to compel by entreaty, as Christ and his apostles did, beseeching men to be reconciled to God. The feast must be filled, and love will leave no lane unsearched to fill it.

So the witnesses read the verse as the gospel reaching to the ends of the earth, and are at one in fencing it from abuse. The highways and hedges are the Gentile world, the homeless and far-off, who too must be brought in. The “compel,” they insist with one voice, is the constraint not of force but of love — the earnest, importunate persuasion of the gospel. The word that persecutors have wrested to justify violence the witnesses reclaim for its true sense: to compel by entreaty, as Christ and his apostles did, beseeching men to be reconciled to God.
24:
On Luke 14:24 — “For I say unto you, That none of those men which were bidden shall taste of my supper” — the witnesses mark the solemn exclusion of the first-bidden.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the words and their scope. Barnes takes them as “the words of Jesus, making an application of the parable” — not that no Jew would be saved, but that “none who had so decidedly rejected the offer of the gospel” would taste it; “we may here see how dangerous it is once to” refuse. Ellicott: the “absolute exclusion” of the first guests “has its anti-type in the general rejection of Israel,” though a parable “deals roughly with general facts.” Robertson keeps it the Master’s summing-up, not shutting the door on individual Jews who might yet turn.

The verse closes the parable with a word of judgement. Those who were first invited and would not come are now barred: they “shall not taste” the supper they despised. The places they scorned are filled by others, and their own refusal has become their exclusion.

So the witnesses read the sober end of the great supper. The grace that gathers the poor and the Gentile is matched by a justice that excludes the contemptuous — not arbitrarily, but as the fruit of their own choice. They would not come; now they may not. The witnesses are careful, with Barnes and Robertson, not to press the parable into a denial of all hope for any individual, but they will not soften its warning: to trifle with the gospel call, to prefer field and oxen and wife to the feast of God, is to risk being shut out for ever. The verse thus seals the lesson of the whole parable — that the kingdom’s supper is free but not to be despised, and that those who make light of the invitation may find, at the last, that they have refused away their place.

So the witnesses read the sober end of the great supper. The grace that gathers the poor and the Gentile is matched by a justice that excludes the contemptuous — not arbitrarily, but as the fruit of their own choice. They would not come; now they may not. The witnesses are careful, with Barnes and Robertson, not to press the parable into a denial of all hope for any individual, but they will not soften its warning: to trifle with the gospel call, preferring field and oxen and wife to the feast of God, is to risk being shut out for ever.
25:
On Luke 14:25 — “And there went great multitudes with him: and he turned, and said unto them” — the witnesses mark the Lord checking the unthinking crowd.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the turn. Gregory: “the mind is kindled when it hears of heavenly rewards… but great rewards cannot be reached except by great labours.” Theophylact: “because many… followed not with their whole heart, but lukewarmly, He shows what kind of a man his disciple ought to be.” The grammatical-historical Robertson reads the dramatic act — “he turned” (strapheis), “a deliberate effort to check the wild and unthinking enthusiasm of the crowds who followed just to be following.” The Puritan Henry: to such zealous followers Christ’s exhortation is “to understand the terms of discipleship before they undertook the profession of it.”

The verse opens the teaching on the cost of discipleship. Great multitudes throng after Jesus, many of them, the witnesses note, with carnal hopes of a temporal kingdom; and the Lord, far from flattering the crowd, turns and presses on them the hard conditions of following him. He would have disciples who count the cost, not camp-followers swept along by enthusiasm.

So the witnesses see the Lord deliberately thin the ranks of the half-hearted. He does not court popularity; he confronts it, turning on the eager multitude to declare what discipleship truly demands. The fathers mark the wisdom of it: heavenly rewards are real, but they are reached only by great labour, and a following that is merely lukewarm will not endure. The witnesses thus open the section on bearing the cross and renouncing all — a teaching meant not to swell the crowd but to test it, that those who would follow might know, before they begin, the cross they must carry and the all they must forsake.

So the witnesses see the Lord deliberately thin the ranks of the half-hearted. He does not court popularity but confronts it, turning on the eager multitude to declare what discipleship truly demands. The fathers mark the wisdom of it: heavenly rewards are real, but reached only by great labour, and a lukewarm following will not endure. The witnesses thus open the section on bearing the cross and renouncing all — a teaching meant not to swell the crowd but to test it, that those who follow might know, before they begin, the cross they must carry and the all they must forsake.
26:
On Luke 14:26 — “If any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother… yea, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple” — the witnesses interpret the hard word “hate.”

The grammatical-historical commentators are unanimous that “hate” means “love less.” Barnes: “the word hate here means simply to love less”; “we are not at liberty literally to hate our parents” (Ephesians 6:1). Clarke cites Matthew’s plainer “loveth… more than me” (Matthew 10:37), and Romans 9:13 (“Esau have I hated” = loved less). Robertson: “the orientals use strong language where cooler spirits would speak of preference”; the hatred is only “where the element of choice comes in” (cf. Matthew 6:24), for Matthew 15:4 commands honour to parents. Ellicott marks the form here is stronger — “not hating” for “loving more” — spoken to the whole multitude.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley sums it: love them “comparatively to Christ… so as actually to renounce… all things… when they stand in competition with him.”

So the witnesses guard the saying from a monstrous literalism while preserving its edge. The Lord does not bid us hate the parents he commands us to honour; he demands that our love for him so surpass every earthly love that, set beside it, the dearest natural affection looks like hatred — and, where they conflict, is decisively subordinated. Even “his own life” must be hated, that is, held cheaper than Christ. The witnesses thus read the verse as the law of supreme allegiance: discipleship is not an addition to our other loves but their Lord, claiming a devotion that, in the hour of choice, prefers Christ to father and mother, wife and child, and life itself. It is a hard word, but not a cruel one — the necessary cost of following a Master who is worthy of all.

So the witnesses guard the saying from a monstrous literalism while preserving its edge. The Lord does not bid us hate the parents he commands us to honour; he demands that our love for him so surpass every earthly love that, set beside it, the dearest natural affection looks like hatred — and, where they conflict, is decisively subordinated. Even “his own life” must be held cheaper than Christ. It is the law of supreme allegiance: discipleship is not an addition to our other loves but their Lord, claiming a devotion that, in the hour of choice, prefers Christ to all — a hard word, but not a cruel one.
27:
On Luke 14:27 — “And whosoever doth not bear his cross, and come after me, cannot be my disciple” — the witnesses mark the second condition of discipleship.

The Eastern fathers spiritualise the cross. Chrysostom: he means not “a beam of wood on our shoulders, but that we should ever have death before our eyes,” as Paul “died daily.” Basil joins it to baptism, where “our old man is crucified.” The grammatical-historical commentators note the familiar figure (Luke 9:23; Matthew 16:24): Robertson, that “each follower has a cross which he must bear as Jesus did his,” the verb of cross-bearing used elsewhere only of Christ (John 19:17); Ellicott, that the word had “a fresh significance” now, the cross of Christ himself “every day more clear and terrible in its growing nearness.”

The verse adds to supreme love the willing endurance of suffering. To follow Christ is to take up a cross — the reproach, affliction, and death that may attend his service — and to carry it after him. There is no discipleship that does not share, in its measure, the Master’s shame and pain.

So the witnesses set the cross beside the renunciation of every rival love as the second great cost. The disciple must not only prefer Christ above all but be ready to suffer for him, bearing his own cross as Christ bore his. The fathers read the cross inwardly — the daily dying to sin and self that begins at baptism; the expositors read it of the real persecutions that following Christ may bring. Both meet in the one demand: a willingness to be crucified with him. The witnesses note how solemnly the words fell as the Lord himself drew near his cross; and they press the lesson that no man is fit to be a disciple who will not, with Christ, take up his cross and carry it to the end.

So the witnesses set the cross beside the renunciation of every rival love as the second great cost. The disciple must not only prefer Christ above all but be ready to suffer for him, bearing his own cross as Christ bore his. The fathers read it inwardly — the daily dying to sin and self that begins at baptism; the expositors, of the real persecutions following Christ may bring. Both meet in one demand: a willingness to be crucified with him. The words fell solemnly as the Lord himself drew near his cross; no man is fit to be a disciple who will not take up his own and carry it to the end.
28:
On Luke 14:28 — “For which of you, intending to build a tower, sitteth not down first, and counteth the cost, whether he have sufficient to finish it?” — the witnesses read the parable of the prudent builder.

The Eastern and Latin fathers draw the lesson. Gregory: “everything that we do should be preceded by anxious consideration. If then we desire to build a tower of humility, we ought first to brace ourselves against the ills of this world.” Theophylact warns against laying a foundation and not finishing, “as those of whom St. John writes, that many of his disciples went back” (John 6:66). Basil presses that the Lord’s aim is not to give “liberty… to become His disciple or not,” but to show “the impossibility of pleasing God amidst those things which distract the soul.” The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the “attitude of deliberation” in “sit down” (cf. Proverbs 24:27).

The Reformed Calvin states the point: “we must consider beforehand what the profession of the gospel demands… The reason why many persons yield to very slight temptations is, that they have pictured to themselves unmixed enjoyment.”

So the witnesses read the parable as the warrant for the hard sayings just spoken. Having named the cost — supreme love and the cross — the Lord bids men reckon it before they begin, as a builder reckons before he builds. To take up discipleship lightly, on a wave of enthusiasm, and then to abandon it when the cost is felt, is to be like the man who lays a foundation he cannot finish, and earns the mockery of all. The witnesses note that the Lord does not lower the cost to keep the crowd; he raises the warning, that those who follow may follow knowingly and to the end. The tower of a Christian profession must be built on a counted cost, lest it stand half-finished as a monument to a rash beginning.

So the witnesses read the parable as the warrant for the hard sayings just spoken. Having named the cost — supreme love and the cross — the Lord bids men reckon it before they begin, as a builder reckons before he builds. To take up discipleship lightly, on a wave of enthusiasm, and abandon it when the cost is felt, is to be the man who lays a foundation he cannot finish, and earns the mockery of all. The Lord does not lower the cost to keep the crowd; he raises the warning, that those who follow may follow knowingly and to the end.
29:
On Luke 14:29 — “Lest haply, after he hath laid the foundation, and is not able to finish it, all that behold it begin to mock him” — the witnesses mark the shame of the unfinished work.

The voices here are brief. The Reformed Gill applies it to the rash professor who “has begun to build, has taken up a profession… got into a church state,” and then fails. The grammatical-historical Barnes: “to mock him” is “to ridicule him, to laugh at him.” Robertson notes the perfective force of “to finish out to the end” (ektelesai), and the two genitive absolutes — the foundation laid, the strength wanting.

The verse presses the warning of the parable with the picture of public ridicule. The builder who begins what he cannot complete leaves a half-built tower for all to laugh at; his failure is not private but exposed to every passer-by. So the disciple who undertakes Christ’s service without counting the cost, and then falls away, becomes a spectacle and a byword.

So the witnesses read in the mockery the dishonour brought on Christ by a half-hearted profession. The unfinished tower is the apostate or the backslider — one who took up religion with zeal and laid it down under trial, leaving his faith a ruin for the world to scorn. The witnesses note the shame that such failure casts not only on the man but on the name he bore: the watching world is quick to mock the professor who cannot persevere. The verse thus drives home the call to count the cost: better not to begin than to begin and abandon; and the disciple who would not be mocked must reckon the whole demand of discipleship and resolve, in God’s strength, to finish what he begins.

So the witnesses read in the mockery the dishonour brought on Christ by a half-hearted profession. The unfinished tower is the apostate or backslider — one who took up religion with zeal and laid it down under trial, leaving his faith a ruin for the world to scorn. The witnesses note the shame such failure casts not only on the man but on the name he bore: the watching world is quick to mock the professor who cannot persevere. Better not to begin than to begin and abandon; the disciple who would not be mocked must reckon the whole demand and resolve, in God’s strength, to finish.
30:
On Luke 14:30 — “Saying, This man began to build, and was not able to finish” — the witnesses mark the verdict of the onlookers.

The voices here are brief. The Reformed Gill paraphrases the rash beginner: he “set out well… promised great things, and made a considerable… stir, as if he would carry things at once to a very high pitch” — and then could not finish. The grammatical-historical Barnes refers to the failure to finish (Hebrews 7:11 on incompleteness), and Robertson notes the contemptuous “this fellow” (houtos ho anthropos).

The verse puts the mockery into words. The onlookers’ taunt — “this man began… and was not able to finish” — is the epitaph of every rash undertaking: a great start, a feeble end. The shame is in the gap between the promise and the performance.

So the witnesses read the world’s scornful judgement on the half-hearted disciple. The taunt fastens not on the smallness of the work but on the failure to complete it: he began. A bold beginning that ends in abandonment is worse than no beginning, for it leaves a standing proof of folly. The witnesses note how the verse warns against the religion of mere beginnings — the zeal that flares and dies, the profession taken up in haste and dropped under pressure. To be a disciple is not to start well but to finish; and the mocking words “was not able to finish” are set down by the Lord as the dread alternative to counting the cost. The wise will so reckon the demand, and so lean on the grace that supplies, that their tower is not begun in vain but built to its top.

So the witnesses read the world’s scornful judgement on the half-hearted disciple. The taunt fastens not on the smallness of the work but on the failure to complete it: he began. A bold beginning that ends in abandonment is worse than none, for it leaves a standing proof of folly. The witnesses warn against the religion of mere beginnings — the zeal that flares and dies, the profession taken up in haste and dropped under pressure. To be a disciple is not to start well but to finish; and the wise will so reckon the demand, and lean on the grace that supplies, that their tower is built to its top.
31:
On Luke 14:31 — “Or what king, going to make war against another king, sitteth not down first, and consulteth whether he be able with ten thousand to meet him that cometh against him with twenty thousand?” — the witnesses read the second parable of counting the cost.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the lesson. Barnes: the king must consider “whether he will be able… to meet his enemy,” and perhaps Christ means that “the enemies which we have to encounter… are many and strong, and that our strength is comparatively feeble.” Ellicott notes a possible “side-glance at contemporary history” — Herod’s ruinous war with Aretas. Robertson parses the military verbs — “to grapple with another king in war.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley asks whether the “another king” means “the prince of this world… he has greater numbers on his side.”

The verse repeats, in a second figure, the call to deliberate before beginning. As the builder must count his money, so the king must count his troops; to march against a stronger foe without reckoning is folly. Discipleship, like war, is no light undertaking, to be entered without measuring the forces ranged against the soul.

So the witnesses set the warring king beside the building man as a double warning. The Christian life is a warfare against powerful enemies — the world, the flesh, the devil — and a feeble, unreckoning beginning will end in defeat. The witnesses note the candour of the Lord: he does not pretend the way is easy or the foe small, but bids the would-be disciple weigh the conflict before he enters it. Yet the parable is not meant to drive men from discipleship but to drive them to count rightly — to see that they cannot, in their own strength, meet the stronger king, and so to seek the terms of peace and the help of God. The next verse will turn the figure toward that very humility, and the whole toward the renunciation that alone makes a disciple able to stand.

So the witnesses set the warring king beside the building man as a double warning. The Christian life is a warfare against powerful enemies — the world, the flesh, the devil — and a feeble, unreckoning beginning will end in defeat. The witnesses note the candour of the Lord: he does not pretend the way easy or the foe small, but bids the would-be disciple weigh the conflict first. Yet the parable is meant not to drive men from discipleship but to drive them to count rightly — to see that they cannot, in their own strength, meet the stronger king, and so to seek the help of God.
32:
On Luke 14:32 — “Or else, while the other is yet a great way off, he sendeth an ambassage, and desireth conditions of peace” — the witnesses read the king’s prudent recourse.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the figure. Barnes cautions against pressing the details too far, the “general scope… to be learned from the connection.” Ellicott reads “conditions of peace” (literally “the things that make for peace,” as Luke 19:42) as teaching “the necessity of thoroughness”: if we cannot wage the war, we must make peace. Robertson notes the old word for “ambassage” (presbeian), the embassy of elders.

The verse completes the parable of the king. The ruler too weak to fight does not march to certain ruin; while his enemy is still far off, he sues for peace. The wisdom lies in facing the truth of his weakness and acting on it, rather than rushing blindly into a war he cannot win.

So the witnesses draw the application with some care. At one level the parable simply repeats the call to count the cost: do not begin what you cannot carry through. But several witnesses see a deeper turn — that the sinner, weak before the demands of God and the warfare of the soul, must, like the outmatched king, seek terms of peace. The way of the disciple is not to trust his own strength against a stronger foe, but to make peace with God and lean on his help. The witnesses thus read the warring king not as a counsel of despair but as a counsel of humility: knowing we cannot stand in our own power, we sue for the peace God offers in the gospel, and so enter discipleship not as self-confident warriors but as those who have first made their peace with the King.

So the witnesses draw the application with care. At one level the parable repeats the call to count the cost; but several see a deeper turn — that the sinner, weak before the demands of God and the warfare of the soul, must, like the outmatched king, seek terms of peace. The way of the disciple is not to trust his own strength against a stronger foe but to make peace with God and lean on his help. The warring king is thus no counsel of despair but of humility: knowing we cannot stand in our own power, we sue for the peace God offers in the gospel.
33:
On Luke 14:33 — “So likewise, whosoever he be of you that forsaketh not all that he hath, he cannot be my disciple” — the witnesses mark the conclusion of the cost.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the renunciation. Robertson notes “renounceth not” (apotassetai), a military word for separating oneself, “say good-bye to all his property.” Ellicott: better “renounceth” — the lesson the eager disciples had to learn, “to say good-bye to their ‘all,’ whatever that might be… possessions… ease… besetting sins… fancied righteousness.” Barnes points to Paul’s counting all things loss (Philippians 3:7–8). Clarke notes it was “particularly” to those who would preach.

The Reformed Calvin states the heart of it: the disciple must so “prefer Christ… both to their own life, and to all the wishes of the flesh, that nothing” deters them; and Wesley, that we forsake all “by withdrawing our affections from all the creatures… enjoying them only in and for God.”

So the witnesses gather the two parables into their conclusion: the cost of discipleship is nothing less than the renunciation of all. To count the cost, like the builder and the king, is to see that following Christ demands the surrender of everything — not necessarily the literal abandonment of property, the witnesses note, but the heart’s release of it, holding all things loosely and Christ supremely. The disciple says good-bye to his “all” — possessions, ambitions, sins, self-righteousness — keeping nothing back as a rival to his Lord. The witnesses thus close the teaching where it began: supreme love, the cross, the counted cost, the forsaken all — the high and unflinching terms on which alone a man may be Christ’s disciple, and which the eager multitude must weigh before they follow.

So the witnesses gather the two parables into their conclusion: the cost of discipleship is nothing less than the renunciation of all. To count the cost, like the builder and the king, is to see that following Christ demands the surrender of everything — not necessarily the literal abandonment of property, but the heart’s release of it, holding all things loosely and Christ supremely. The disciple says good-bye to his “all” — possessions, ambitions, sins, self-righteousness — keeping nothing back as a rival to his Lord. These are the high, unflinching terms on which alone a man may be Christ’s disciple.
34:
On Luke 14:34 — “Salt is good: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be seasoned?” — the witnesses read the closing parable of the salt.

The Latin and Eastern fathers draw the meaning. Bede connects it to the unfinished tower: “it is a good thing to season the secrets of the heart with the salt of spiritual wisdom,” even to become “the salt of the earth.” Theophylact extends it beyond teachers to “private individuals also… useful to those around them,” warning that “if he who is to be useful to others becomes reprobate, he cannot be profited.” The grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott set it beside Matthew 5:13 and Mark 9:50, salt as “the purifying element… the principle of unselfish devotion”; Lightfoot reads it of the apostate who, having weighed the cost and gone back, becomes “unsavoury salt.”

The verse applies the figure of salt to the disciple. Salt is good and useful — but salt that has lost its savour is good for nothing. So the disciple who has lost the inward grace that made him useful — the spiritual savour of true devotion — is fit for no purpose at all.

So the witnesses read the salt as the seal of the whole teaching on discipleship. To begin and not finish, to profess and then go back, is to become like savourless salt: a thing that was meant to season and preserve, now useless and cast out. The fathers apply it to every Christian, not teachers only, called to be “useful to those around them” by the salt of grace. The witnesses press the solemn warning: the lapsed disciple is in the saddest of states, for as savourless salt cannot itself be re-salted, so the one who has tasted and renounced the grace of Christ is hard to recover. The verse thus closes the call to count the cost with a picture of the ruin of those who do not — salt grown tasteless, fit only to be thrown away.

So the witnesses read the salt as the seal of the teaching on discipleship. To begin and not finish, to profess and go back, is to become like savourless salt: a thing meant to season and preserve, now useless and cast out. The fathers apply it to every Christian, not teachers only, called to be “useful to those around them” by the salt of grace. The witnesses press the solemn warning: the lapsed disciple is in the saddest of states, for as savourless salt cannot be re-salted, so the one who has tasted and renounced the grace of Christ is hard to recover.
35:
On Luke 14:35 — “It is neither fit for the land, nor yet for the dunghill; but men cast it out. He that hath ears to hear, let him hear” — the witnesses mark the uselessness of the savourless and the closing call.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the new use of the figure. Ellicott: this illustration, “differing… from that in Matthew 5:13 and Mark 9:50, proves the independence of the saying” — salt fit neither for the soil nor even for the dunghill, “a lower and humbler use” than seasoning. Barnes: it “will not bear fruit… nor… good for manure… men cast it out.” Robertson notes the impersonal “men cast it out,” and that “he that hath ears” is another of Christ’s repeated sayings (Luke 8:8; Matthew 11:15).

The verse drives the warning of the salt to its end. Savourless salt is doubly worthless — useless to enrich the land, useless even on the dunghill; there is nothing for it but to be thrown away. And to this dread picture the Lord appends his solemn summons: let him that hath ears hear — mark this well.

So the witnesses close the chapter on the most searching of warnings, sealed by the call to attend. The disciple who loses his savour — who counts not the cost, begins and does not finish, professes and falls away — is fit for nothing but to be cast out, like salt that can neither season food, fertilise the field, nor even be safely heaped on the dunghill. The witnesses note the gravity of the closing formula: “He that hath ears to hear, let him hear” is the Lord’s way of pressing a hard truth on the conscience. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem hears in the “ears” the new hearing given in baptism for the divine mysteries. The whole teaching on discipleship thus ends with a plea for serious attention: weigh the cost, hold fast the savour, and hear — lest, having begun, you be cast out at the last.

So the witnesses close the chapter on the most searching of warnings, sealed by the call to attend. The disciple who loses his savour — who counts not the cost, begins and does not finish, professes and falls away — is fit for nothing but to be cast out, like salt useless for soil or dunghill alike. The witnesses note the gravity of the closing formula, “He that hath ears to hear, let him hear” — the Lord’s way of pressing a hard truth on the conscience. The whole teaching ends with a plea for serious attention: weigh the cost, hold fast the savour, and hear.
